statement: Chemistry is the study of the composition, properties, and interactions of matter.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Chemistry is sometimes referred to as the central science due to its interconnectedness with other disciplines.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The practice of chemistry is not limited to chemistry books or laboratories, but happens whenever someone is involved in changes in matter or conditions that may lead to such changes.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The scientific method is a process that follows a route of observation, hypothesis, experimentation, and theory to answer questions and explain observations.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Chemists study and describe the behavior of matter and energy in three different domains: macroscopic, microscopic, and symbolic.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The macroscopic domain is the realm of everyday things that are large enough to be sensed directly by human sight or touch.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The microscopic domain is the realm of small entities such as molecules and atoms that are too small to be seen.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The symbolic domain contains the specialized language used to represent components of the macroscopic and microscopic domains.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Matter is defined as anything that occupies space and has mass.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Solids, liquids, and gases are the three states of matter commonly found on earth.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: A solid is rigid and possesses a definite shape.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: A liquid flows and takes the shape of its container.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: A gas takes both the shape and volume of its container.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Weight refers to the force that gravity exerts on an object.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The weight of an object changes as the force of gravity changes, but its mass does not.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The law of conservation of matter states that there is no detectable change in the total quantity of matter present when matter converts from one type to another.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: A pure substance has a constant composition and exhibits the same properties throughout.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: An element is a pure substance that cannot be broken down into simpler substances by chemical changes.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: A compound is a pure substance that is comprised of two or more elements and can be broken down by chemical changes.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: A mixture is composed of two or more types of matter that can be present in varying amounts and can be separated by physical changes.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: A heterogeneous mixture is a mixture with a composition that varies from point to point.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: A homogeneous mixture, also called a solution, exhibits a uniform composition and appears visually the same throughout.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: An atom is the smallest particle of an element that has the properties of that element and can enter into a chemical combination.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The concept of atoms was first suggested by the Greek philosophers Leucippus and Democritus in the 5th century.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: John Dalton supported the atomic hypothesis with quantitative measurements in the early 19th century.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: A molecule consists of two or more atoms joined by strong forces called chemical bonds.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: A water molecule is a unit that contains two hydrogen atoms and one oxygen atom.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The decomposition of water into hydrogen and oxygen gases can be achieved through the addition of energy, such as with a battery or power supply.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: A physical property is a characteristic of matter that is not associated with a change in its chemical composition.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The melting of wax is an example of a physical change, as it does not involve a change in the chemical identities of the substances contained in the matter.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: A chemical change always produces one or more types of matter that differ from the matter present before the change.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The formation of rust is a chemical change because rust is a different kind of matter than the iron, oxygen, and water present before the rust formed.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: An extensive property is a property that depends on the amount of matter present.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The mass and volume of a substance are examples of extensive properties.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Temperature is an example of an intensive property.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The periodic table is a table of elements that places elements with similar properties close together.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: A measurement provides three kinds of information: the size or magnitude of the measurement, a standard of comparison for the measurement, and an indication of the uncertainty of the measurement.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The International System of Units (SI Units) is a system of units that has been adopted by the United States National Institute of Standards and Technology.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The maximum takeoff weight of a Boeing 767 airliner is 186,000 kilograms, which can also be written as 1.86 x 10^5 kg.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The standard unit of length in the metric system is the meter (m).
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: One meter is about 39.37 inches or 1.094 yards.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The standard unit of mass in the metric system is the kilogram (kg).
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: One kilogram is about 2.2046 pounds.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The standard unit of temperature in the metric system is the kelvin (K).
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Celsius degrees are the same magnitude as those of kelvin, but the two scales place their zeros in different places.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The standard unit of time in the metric system is the second (s).
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Small and large time intervals can be expressed with the appropriate prefixes, such as microseconds and megaseconds.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Volume is the measure of the amount of space occupied by an object.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The standard unit of volume is the cubic meter (m^3), which is a cube with an edge length of exactly one meter.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Density is the ratio of the mass of a sample of a substance to its volume.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The density of lead can be calculated by dividing its mass by its volume, which is calculated by cubing the edge length of a cube of lead.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Measurement uncertainty refers to the degree of doubt associated with the result of a measurement.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Counting is the only type of measurement that is free from uncertainty.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Accuracy refers to how close a measurement is to the true value.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Precision refers to how close the measurements are to each other.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Significant figures are the digits in a measurement that are known to be reliable and certain.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: When adding or subtracting numbers, round the result to the same number of decimal places as the number with the least number of decimal places.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: When multiplying or dividing numbers, round the result to the same number of digits as the number with the least number of significant figures.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Captive zeros are always significant figures because they result from measurement.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Leading zeros are never significant figures because they merely tell us where the decimal point is located.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: To determine the number of significant figures in a measurement, count the digits starting from the first non-zero digit on the left.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The number 7.83 has three significant figures because the digits 7, 8, and 3 are all reliable and certain.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Every measurement has some degree of uncertainty, which depends on the device used and the user's ability.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: To round a number to a certain number of significant figures, look at the digit to the right of the last significant figure and decide whether to round up or down based on its value.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Measurements are said to be precise if they yield very similar results when repeated in the same manner
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Accurate values agree with a true value
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: To determine the density of a material, divide its mass by its volume
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The units of quantities must be subjected to the same mathematical operations as their associated numbers
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Dimensional analysis is based on the premise that the units of quantities must be subjected to the same mathematical operations as their associated numbers
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The density of iron is approximately 7.9 g/cm^3
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: To convert a volume of 3.78 qt to liters, use the conversion factor: 1 qt = 0.946 L
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: To convert a mass from pounds to grams, use the conversion factor: 1 lb = 453.6 g
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: To calculate the density of common antifreeze, divide its mass in grams by its volume in milliliters
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Temperature is a measure of the hotness or coldness of a substance.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The Celsius scale is a temperature scale where water freezes at 0°C and boils at 100°C.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The Fahrenheit scale is a temperature scale where water freezes at 32°F and boils at 212°F.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The kelvin scale is an absolute temperature scale where 0 K is the lowest temperature that can be achieved.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: To convert a temperature from Celsius to Fahrenheit, use the equation: y = (9/5)x + 32
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: To convert a temperature from Celsius to kelvin, use the equation: K = x + 273.15
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Normal body temperature is 37°C, which is equal to 98.6°F and 310.15 K.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The freezing temperature of water on the kelvin scale is 273.15 K.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The boiling temperature of water on the kelvin scale is 373.15 K.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Chemical properties are characteristics of a substance that involve a change in the composition of the substance, such as flammability and acidity.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Extensive properties depend on the amount of matter present, while intensive properties do not depend on the amount of matter present.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Density is an intensive property that is defined as the ratio of mass to volume.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The law of conservation of matter states that matter cannot be created or destroyed in a chemical reaction.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: To convert a measured quantity from one unit to another, use unit conversion factors derived from simple applications of the factor-label method or dimensional analysis.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The density of a substance can be calculated by dividing its mass by its volume, such as the density of gold.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The formula for density is density = mass / volume.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The unit of density in the metric system is grams per cubic centimeter (g/cm^3) or kilograms per liter (kg/L).
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The water displacement method can be used to measure the mass and volume of an object.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The density of a substance can be used to determine its identity.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Atoms of one element differ in properties from atoms of all other elements.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Atoms are neither created nor destroyed during a chemical change, but are instead rearranged to yield substances that are different from those present before the change.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Dalton's atomic theory provides a microscopic explanation of the many macroscopic properties of matter.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: A compound consists of atoms of two or more elements combined in a small, whole-number ratio.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The combination of copper and oxygen atoms in a 1:1 ratio results in the formation of a compound, copper oxide.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: An element consists of only one type of atom, which has a mass that is characteristic of the element and is the same for all atoms of that element.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The law of definite proportions states that all samples of a pure compound contain the same elements in the same proportion by mass.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The ratio of the masses of elements in a compound is always a small, whole-number ratio.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The law of multiple proportions states that when two elements react to form more than one compound, a fixed mass of one element will react with masses of the other element in a ratio of small, whole numbers.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Copper and chlorine can form a green, crystalline solid with a mass ratio of 1:3.53g chlorine to 1g copper, and a brown crystalline solid with a mass ratio of 2:1g chlorine to 1g copper.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: An electron is a negatively charged, subatomic particle with a mass more than one thousand times less than that of an atom.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The charge of an electron is 1.602 x 10^-19 C.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Millikan's oil drop experiment measured the charge of individual oil drops and found that the charge was always a multiple of a specific charge, which is the charge of a single electron.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The plum pudding model of atoms describes a positively charged mass with an equal amount of negative charge in the form of electrons embedded in it.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The volume occupied by an atom consists of a large amount of empty space.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: A small, relatively heavy, positively charged body, the nucleus, must be at the center of each atom.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Isotopes are atoms of the same element that differ in mass.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The number of protons in the nucleus of an atom is its atomic number (Z).
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The mass number (A) is the total number of protons and neutrons in an atom.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The atomic mass unit (amu) is defined as one-twelfth the mass of a carbon-12 atom.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: A neutral atom must contain the same number of positive and negative charges.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: An ion is an atom that is electrically charged due to the gain or loss of electrons.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Iodine atoms are added as anions with a -1 charge and a mass number of 127.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: An element's symbol is a unique abbreviation for that element.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Only the first letter of a symbol is capitalized.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The mass of an atom is approximately equal to its mass number.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: To calculate the average atomic mass of an element, multiply each isotope's mass by its fractional abundance and sum the results.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The average mass of a neon atom in the solar wind is 20.179 amu, which is different from the average mass of a terrestrial neon atom (20.183 amu).
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The atomic number of an element defines its identity and is equal to the number of protons in an atom's nucleus.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Chlorine consists of 75.78% Cl-35 and 24.22% Cl-37.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The symbol for a specific isotope of an element is written by placing the mass number as a superscript to the left of the element symbol.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: A molecular formula is a representation of a molecule that uses chemical symbols to indicate the types of atoms followed by subscripts to show the number of atoms of each type in the molecule.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: A structural formula for a compound gives the same information as its molecular formula, the types and numbers of atoms in the molecule, but also shows how the atoms are connected in the molecule.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The expression of a molecular formula, such as H2, represents a diatomic molecule of hydrogen, consisting of two atoms of the element that are chemically bonded together.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: An empirical formula indicates the types of atoms present and the simplest whole-number ratio of the number of atoms, or ions, in the compound.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The molecular formula for benzene is C6H6, which can be determined experimentally by finding the molecular mass and the empirical formula, CH.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Compounds with the same molecular formula may have different atom-to-atom bonding and therefore different structures, known as isomers.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Acetic acid and methyl formate are structural isomers, with the same formula, C2H4O2, but different structures and chemical properties.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The periodic law states that the properties of the elements are periodic functions of their atomic numbers.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The modern periodic table arranges the elements in increasing order of their atomic numbers and groups atoms with similar properties in the same vertical column.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Elements in the same group of the periodic table have similar chemical properties due to the same number of electrons in their outer shell.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The elements in group 1, such as lithium, sodium, and potassium, are known as alkali metals and have similar chemical properties.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The elements can be classified into main group elements, transition metals, and inner transition metals based on their properties and position in the periodic table.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Metals are elements that are shiny, malleable, and good conductors of heat and electricity.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Nonmetals are elements that appear dull and are poor conductors of heat and electricity.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Metalloids are elements that conduct heat and electricity moderately well and possess some properties of metals and some properties of nonmetals.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Ionic compounds are compounds that contain ions and are held together by ionic bonds.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: When a metal is combined with one or more nonmetals, the compound is usually ionic.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Ionic compounds are solids that typically melt at high temperatures and boil at even higher temperatures.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: In an ionic compound, the total number of positive charges of the cations equals the total number of negative charges of the anions.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Sodium chloride (NaCl) is an ionic compound composed of sodium ions (Na+) and chloride ions (Cl-).
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Covalent bonds are the attractive forces between the positively charged nuclei of the bonded atoms and one or more pairs of electrons that are located between the atoms.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The periodic table can help predict the formation of ionic compounds by identifying metals and nonmetals.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The reaction between magnesium (Mg) and nitrogen (N) forms an ionic compound, with magnesium forming a cation (Mg2+) and nitrogen forming an anion (N3-).
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The formula of an ionic compound must have a ratio of ions such that the numbers of positive and negative charges are equal.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Polyatomic ions are ions formed from more than one atom, and they can be either positive or negative.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Molecular compounds often exist as gases, low-boiling liquids, and low-melting solids.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Nomenclature refers to a collection of rules for naming things.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The name of a binary compound containing monatomic ions consists of the name of the cation followed by the name of the anion with its ending replaced by the suffix –ide.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The formula for calcium phosphate is Ca₃(PO₄)₂, indicating three calcium ions and two phosphate groups.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The periodic table can help us recognize many of the compounds that are covalent.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The formula for sodium oxalate is Na₂C₂O₄, which is not an empirical formula.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: An ionic hydrate is a compound that contains water molecules and is named by appending a vertically centered dot, a coefficient representing the number of water molecules, and the formula for water.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: To name ionic compounds, the charges of cations and anions must be considered to determine the correct ratios of ions in the compound.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The name of the more metallic element in a molecular compound is given first, followed by the name of the more nonmetallic element with its ending changed to the suffix -ide.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: To name a molecular compound composed of two nonmetallic elements, use Greek prefixes to specify the numbers of atoms of each element.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The compound CO is named carbon monoxide, and the compound CO2 is named carbon dioxide.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The compound H2O is commonly known as water, not dihydrogen monoxide.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: To name a binary acid, change the word 'hydrogen' to the prefix 'hydro-', modify the other nonmetallic element name by adding the suffix '-ic', and add the word 'acid' as a second word.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The gas HCl is dissolved in water to form hydrochloric acid.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: To name an oxyacid, omit 'hydrogen', start with the root name of the anion, replace '-ate' with '-ic' or '-ite' with '-ous', and add 'acid'.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The compound H2CO3 is named carbonic acid.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: An actinide is an inner transition metal in the bottom two rows of the periodic table.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: An alpha particle is a positively charged particle consisting of two protons and two neutrons.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: An anion is a negatively charged atom or molecule that contains more electrons than protons.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Atomic mass is the average mass of atoms of an element, expressed in atomic mass units (amu).
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: A binary acid is a compound that contains hydrogen and one other element, bonded in a way that imparts acidic properties to the compound.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: A cation is a positively charged atom or molecule that contains fewer electrons than protons.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Covalent compounds are composed of molecules formed by atoms of two or more different elements.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The fundamental unit of charge is the magnitude of the charge of an electron, denoted as e.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: A molecular compound is composed of molecules formed by atoms of two or more different elements.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The molecular formula of a compound indicates the exact number of atoms of each element in a molecule of the compound.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: To name a binary ionic compound, the name of the metal is written first, followed by the name of the nonmetal with its ending changed to -ide.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The symbol for the ion with a 3+ charge, atomic number 13, and mass number 27 is 27Al3+.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The average atomic mass of an element can vary depending on the source of the element.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The periodic table can be used to classify elements as metals or nonmetals and to identify the lightest or heaviest member of a group.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The symbol for a neutral isotope includes the atomic number and mass number.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Compounds can be classified as ionic or covalent based on the elements involved.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The molecular and empirical formulas of a compound can be determined from its composition.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Formula mass is the sum of the average atomic masses of all the atoms represented in the substance’s formula.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The molecular mass of a chloroform molecule is 153.823 amu, which is the sum of the average atomic masses of its constituent atoms.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The molecular mass of an aspirin molecule is the sum of the atomic masses of nine carbon atoms, eight hydrogen atoms, and four oxygen atoms, which amounts to 180.157 amu.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The formula mass of a covalent substance represents the numbers and types of atoms composing a single molecule of the substance.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The mole concept is a unit of amount that represents 6.022 x 10^23 particles, such as atoms or molecules.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The formula mass of an ionic compound is calculated in the same way as the formula mass for covalent compounds, by summing the average atomic masses of all the atoms in the compound’s formula.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The formula mass of sodium chloride (NaCl) is computed as 22.99 + 35.45 = 58.44 amu.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The mass of an electron is negligibly small with respect to the mass of a typical atom.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: One mole of any element contains the same number of atoms as one mole of any other element.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The molar mass of aluminum sulfate (Al2(SO4)3) is calculated as (2 * 26.98) + (3 * 32.07) + (12 * 16.00) = 342.14 g/mol.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The molar mass of a substance is numerically equivalent to its atomic or formula weight in amu.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The molar amount of a substance may be calculated by dividing its mass (g) by its molar mass (g/mol).
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: A 100g sample of copper wire contains approximately 3.46 * 10^23 copper atoms.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: To derive the number of moles of a compound from its mass, divide the mass by the molar mass of the compound.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The mass of 3.44 * 10^-3 mol of gold is approximately 664g.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: To derive the number of atoms of an element from its mass, first calculate the molar amount of the element, then use Avogadro’s number to convert this molar amount to number of atoms.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The molar mass of a compound is computed by summing the atomic masses of its constituent atoms.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The number of moles of glycine molecules in a 75g sample of glycine is approximately 0.5 mol.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The mass of 3.44 * 10^-5 mol of vitamin C is approximately 1.2g.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The number of molecules in a given mass of compound is computed by first deriving the number of moles, then multiplying by Avogadro’s number.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Percent composition is defined as the percentage by mass of each element in a compound.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: A gaseous compound composed solely of carbon and hydrogen has a percent composition of 75.0% C and 25.0% H by mass.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: To derive the empirical formula, first calculate the number of moles of each element from its mass, then divide each element's molar amount by the smallest molar amount to yield subscripts for a tentative empirical formula.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The empirical formula of a compound containing 1.28 g of carbon and 2.16 g of hydrogen is CH2.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The empirical formula of hematite is Fe2O3.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Empirical formulas are symbols representing the relative numbers of a compound’s elements
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The empirical formula mass is the sum of the average atomic masses of all the atoms represented in an empirical formula
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Molarity is defined as the number of moles of solute in exactly 1 liter of the solution
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: To calculate the molar concentration of a solute, use the formula M = moles of solute / liters of solution
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: A 247-mL soft drink sample contains 4.87 mol of sucrose, so the molar concentration of sucrose is 4.87 mol / 0.247 L = 19.7 M
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: A solution in which water is the solvent is called an aqueous solution
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Concentration is the relative amount of a given solution component
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Calculate the molarity of 25.0 g of CoCl2 in an aqueous solution with a total volume of 150.0 mL.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: When performing calculations stepwise, it is important to refrain from rounding any intermediate calculation results to avoid rounding errors in the final result.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Dilution is the process whereby the concentration of a solution is lessened by the addition of solvent.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The dilution equation is given by M1V1 = M2V2, where M1 and V1 are the concentration and volume of the solution before dilution, and M2 and V2 are the concentration and volume after dilution.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: If 25.0 mL of a 0.40 M solution of copper nitrate is diluted to a volume of 100.0 mL, what is the molarity of the diluted solution?
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The dilution equation can be rearranged to isolate the unknown property, such as the concentration of the diluted solution or the volume of the diluted solution.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The mass percentage of a solution component is defined as the ratio of the component's mass to the solution's mass, expressed as a percentage.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: A 100g sample of spinal fluid contains 7.9mg of glucose. What is the percent by mass of glucose in spinal fluid?
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: To calculate the mass of solute in a solution from its mass percentage, the mass of the solution must be known.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Volume percentage is defined as the ratio of a solute's volume to the total solution volume, expressed as a percentage.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Rubbing alcohol is usually sold as a 70%vol aqueous solution of isopropyl alcohol. If the density of isopropyl alcohol is 0.79g/mL, how many grams of isopropyl alcohol are present in a 100mL bottle of rubbing alcohol?
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Physiological saline solution has a concentration of 0.9% mass/volume, indicating that the composition is 9g of solute per 1000mL of solution.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Parts per million (ppm) and parts per billion (ppb) are units used to express very low solute concentrations.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: According to the EPA, when the concentration of lead in tap water reaches 15ppb, certain remedial actions must be taken. What is this concentration in ppm?
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Avogadro’s number (NA): the experimentally determined value of the number of entities comprising one mole of substance, equal to 6.022 x 10^23 mol^-1
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Molar mass: the mass in grams of one mole of a substance
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Parts per billion (ppb): the ratio of solute to solution mass multiplied by 10^9
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Calculate the molar mass of hydrogen fluoride (HF) using its molecular formula: HF = 1.008 g/mol (H) + 19.00 g/mol (F) = 20.008 g/mol
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Molarity is a measure of concentration that is independent of temperature and pressure.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The volume of the solution must be in liters when calculating molarity.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: A 1 M solution of a substance contains 1 mole of that substance per liter of solution.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: If 2 moles of NaCl are dissolved in 4 liters of water, the molarity of the solution is 0.5 M.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: A chemical equation is a symbolic representation of a chemical reaction, where reactants are shown on the left and products on the right, with coefficients representing the relative amounts of each substance.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: A chemical equation must be balanced, meaning that the number of atoms of each element is the same on both the reactant and product sides.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Coefficients in a chemical equation represent the relative numbers of molecules of each substance, and can be interpreted as ratios.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: To balance a chemical equation, count the number of atoms of each element on both the reactant and product sides, and adjust the coefficients as needed to achieve balance.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The reaction between methane (CH4) and oxygen (O2) to produce carbon dioxide (CO2) and water (H2O) can be represented by the balanced equation: CH4 + 2O2 -> CO2 + 2H2O
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The law of conservation of matter requires that a chemical equation be balanced, as the number of atoms of each element cannot change during a chemical reaction.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: A conventional balanced equation with integer-only coefficients is derived by multiplying each coefficient by the smallest whole-number.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The physical states of reactants and products in chemical equations are indicated with a parenthetical abbreviation following the formulas.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Ionic compounds dissolved in water may dissociate into their constituent ions, which are subsequently dispersed homogenously throughout the resulting solution.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: To write a balanced molecular equation, identify formulas for the reactants and products and arrange them properly in chemical equation form.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The solubility of a substance is quantitatively expressed as its maximum concentration that can be achieved under specified conditions.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: A precipitation reaction occurs when dissolved substances react to form one or more solid products.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The solubility guidelines in Table 4.1 may be used to predict whether a precipitation reaction will occur when solutions of soluble ionic compounds are mixed together.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: When solutions of potassium iodide and lead nitrate are mixed, a precipitation reaction occurs, resulting in the formation of solid lead iodide.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: An acid-base reaction is one in which a hydrogen ion is transferred from one chemical species to another.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: An acid is a substance that will dissolve in water to yield hydronium ions.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: A base is a substance that will dissolve in water to yield hydroxide ions.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Strong acids completely react with water to produce hydronium ions.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Hydrogen chloride is a strong acid that completely reacts with water to produce hydronium ions and chloride ions.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Acetic acid is a weak acid that only partially reacts with water to produce hydronium ions and acetate ions.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: A neutralization reaction is a specific type of acid-base reaction in which the reactants are an acid and a base, and the products are often a salt and water.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: To write a balanced chemical equation for an acid-base reaction, identify the reactants and products, and balance the equation.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The reaction between barium hydroxide and nitric acid is a neutralization reaction that produces water and a salt.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Antacids are bases that can neutralize stomach acid and relieve heartburn.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Oxidation-reduction (redox) reactions are those in which one or more elements involved undergo a change in oxidation number.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The oxidation number of an atom in an elemental substance is zero.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The oxidation number of a monatomic ion is equal to the ion’s charge.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Oxidation numbers for common nonmetals are usually assigned as follows: Hydrogen = +1 when combined with nonmetals, -1 when combined with metals; Oxygen = -2 in most compounds.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: To balance redox reactions via the half-reaction method, first write the two half-reactions representing the redox process.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Balance all elements except oxygen and hydrogen in the half-reactions.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Balance oxygen atoms by adding H2O molecules to the half-reactions.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Balance hydrogen atoms by adding H+ ions to the half-reactions.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Balance charge by adding electrons to the half-reactions.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The reaction between sodium and chlorine to yield sodium chloride is a redox reaction, where sodium is oxidized and chlorine is reduced.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The reaction between molecular hydrogen and chlorine is a redox reaction, where hydrogen is oxidized and chlorine is reduced.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The combustion reaction of carbon and oxygen to produce carbon dioxide is a redox reaction, where carbon is oxidized and oxygen is reduced.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Redox reactions can be classified into different subclasses, including combustion reactions and single-displacement reactions.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Stoichiometry is the quantitative assessment of the relationships between the amounts of substances consumed and produced by a chemical reaction.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The coefficients in a balanced equation provide the relative numbers of chemical species.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: To balance a chemical equation, first balance all elements except oxygen and hydrogen, then balance oxygen atoms by adding water molecules, and finally balance hydrogen atoms by adding hydrogen ions.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The balanced equation for the reaction between dichromate ion and iron(II) to yield iron(III) and chromium(III) in acidic solution is: Cr2O72- + 6Fe2+ + 14H+ → 2Cr3+ + 6Fe3+ + 7H2O
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The general approach to using stoichiometric relationships is similar to following a recipe for making a certain number of servings of a dish.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: To determine the amount of one reactant required to react with a given amount of another reactant, use the coefficients in the balanced equation to derive stoichiometric factors.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: To find the number of moles of iodine required to react with 2.55 mol of aluminum, use the balanced equation 2Al + 3I2 → 2AlI3 and the stoichiometric factor 3/2.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The number of molecules of a substance can be calculated using the stoichiometric factor and Avogadro's number.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: To find the mass of sodium hydroxide required to produce 50.0 g of magnesium hydroxide, use the balanced equation 2NaOH + MgCl2 → Mg(OH)2 + 2NaCl and the molar masses of the substances.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Theoretical yield is the amount of product that may be produced by a reaction under specified conditions, as calculated per the stoichiometry of an appropriate balanced chemical equation.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Percent yield is the extent to which a reaction's theoretical yield is achieved, calculated as (actual yield / theoretical yield) * 100.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: To identify the limiting reactant, compare the provided molar amounts of reactants to the stoichiometric amounts represented in the balanced chemical equation.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: When 6.00 g of Si and 8.00 g of N2 react, the limiting reactant is Si because it is provided in a less-than-stoichiometric amount.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The reaction of 3.00 g of CuSO4 with excess Zn metal produces 1.46 g of Cu metal, and the percent yield can be calculated using the theoretical yield and actual yield.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The amount of product obtained is often less than the theoretical yield due to side reactions, incomplete reactions, or loss of product during collection.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The atom economy of a process is a measure of its efficiency, defined as the percentage by mass of the final product of a synthesis relative to the masses of all the reactants used.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The percent yield of a given chemical process evaluates the efficiency of a process by comparing the yield of product actually obtained to the maximum yield predicted by stoichiometry.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The synthesis of ibuprofen using a six-step process requires 794g of reactants to generate each mole, resulting in an atom economy of 41%, while an alternative three-step process has an atom economy of ~77%.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Titration analysis is a quantitative analysis technique that involves the use of a buret to make incremental additions of a solution containing a known concentration of some substance (the titrant) to a sample solution containing the substance whose concentration is to be measured (the analyte).
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: To calculate the molarity of a solution using titration, first calculate the molar amount of the analyte, then use the provided volume of the analyte solution and the definition of molarity to find the concentration.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: A 25.0 mL sample of aqueous HCl was titrated with 42.5 mL of 0.125 M NaOH titrant, and the titration reaction is: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H2O. The molarity of the HCl solution can be calculated using the volume of the titrant and the stoichiometry of the reaction.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Gravimetric analysis is a quantitative analysis technique in which a sample is subjected to some treatment that causes a change in the physical state of the analyte, permitting its separation from the other components of the sample.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Acid: substance that produces H+ when dissolved in water
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Base: substance that produces OH- when dissolved in water
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Combustion analysis: gravimetric technique used to determine the elemental composition of a compound via the collection and weighing of its gaseous combustion products
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Gravimetric analysis: quantitative chemical analysis method involving the separation of an analyte from a sample by a physical or chemical process and subsequent mass measurements of the analyte, reaction product, and/or sample
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Limiting reactant: reactant present in an amount lower than required by the reaction stoichiometry, thus limiting the amount of product generated
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Oxidation number: the charge each atom of an element would have in a compound if the compound were ionic
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Precipitation reaction: reaction that produces one or more insoluble products, when reactants are ionic compounds, sometimes called double displacement or metathesis
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Quantitative analysis: the determination of the amount or concentration of a substance in a sample
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: To determine the concentration of Cl- in a sample, 3.87g of the sample produces 3.44g of AgCl when treated with excess AgNO3
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Combustion analysis of a 4.46g sample of polyethylene yields 4.47g of CO2 and 4.95g of H2O
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: To write a balanced chemical equation, first write the unbalanced equation with the reactants on the left and the products on the right, then adjust the coefficients to balance the equation.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The law of conservation of mass applies to all chemical reactions, meaning that the total mass of the reactants is equal to the total mass of the products.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Chemical reactions can be classified into different types, including synthesis, decomposition, single displacement, and double displacement reactions.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The reaction of sodium with chlorine to form sodium chloride is an example of a synthesis reaction: 2Na + Cl2 → 2NaCl
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: To determine the number of moles and mass of a substance in a reaction, write the balanced equation and outline the steps necessary to determine the requested information.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Energy can be defined as the capacity to supply heat or do work
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Energy comes in different types, including potential energy and kinetic energy
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The law of conservation of energy states that energy can be neither created nor destroyed, although it can be changed in form
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Thermal energy is kinetic energy associated with the random motion of atoms and molecules
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Chemical energy stored in the molecules that compose gasoline is converted into mechanical energy when gasoline is combusted within the cylinders of a car's engine
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Heat is the transfer of thermal energy between two bodies at different temperatures.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Increasing the amount of thermal energy in a sample of matter will cause its temperature to increase, and decreasing the amount of thermal energy will cause its temperature to decrease.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Most substances expand as their temperature increases and contract as their temperature decreases.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The heat capacity of a body of matter is the quantity of heat it absorbs or releases when it experiences a temperature change of 1 degree Celsius or 1 Kelvin.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The specific heat capacity of a substance is the quantity of heat required to raise the temperature of 1 gram of the substance by 1 degree Celsius or 1 Kelvin.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: To calculate the amount of heat absorbed by a substance, use the equation q = c * m * ΔT, where c is the specific heat of the substance, m is its mass, and ΔT is the temperature change.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The unit of heat, work, and energy is the joule (J), which is defined as the amount of energy used when a force of 1 newton moves an object 1 meter.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: An exothermic process is a chemical reaction or physical change that releases heat, while an endothermic process is one that absorbs heat.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Calorimetry is a technique used to measure the amount of heat involved in a chemical or physical process.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The relationship between heat, specific heat, mass, and temperature change can be used to determine any of these quantities if the other three are known or can be deduced.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: To determine the specific heat of a metal, we can use the equation q = mcΔT, where q is the heat added, m is the mass of the metal, c is the specific heat, and ΔT is the temperature change.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Solar thermal systems provide one possible solution to the problem of converting energy from the sun into energy we can use.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The temperature change, along with the specific heat and mass of the solution, can be used to calculate the amount of heat involved in a chemical or physical process.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The Ivanpah Solar Generating System uses 170,000 mirrors to concentrate sunlight on water-filled towers, producing steam that drives electricity-producing turbines.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Calorimetry measurements are important in understanding the heat transferred in reactions involving everything from microscopic proteins to massive machines.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The amount of heat absorbed by the calorimeter is often small enough that we can neglect it.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Energy is neither created nor destroyed during a chemical reaction.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The specific heat of water is approximately 4.184 J/g°C.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: To calculate the heat of a reaction, we can use the equation q = mcΔT, where q is the heat, m is the mass, c is the specific heat, and ΔT is the temperature change.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: When 50.0 g of 0.100 M NaOH(aq) and 50.0 g of 0.100 M HCl(aq), both at 22.0°C, are mixed in a coffee cup calorimeter, the temperature of the mixture reaches a maximum of 28.9°C.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The heat produced or consumed in a reaction equals the amount of heat absorbed or lost by the solution.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: When 6.00 g of solid ammonium nitrate dissolves in 150.0 g of water at 24.9°C in a calorimeter, the temperature decreases to 18.9°C.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The process of crystallization of sodium acetate is exothermic and produces heat that can be absorbed by the hands.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Internal energy is the total of all possible kinds of energy present in a substance.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Energy is stored in a substance when the kinetic energy of its atoms or molecules is raised.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The first law of thermodynamics states that the internal energy of a system changes through heat flow into or out of the system, or work done on or by the system.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Enthalpy is a state function that represents the total energy of a system, including internal energy and the energy associated with the pressure and volume of a system.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The equation ΔU = q + w represents the relationship between internal energy, heat, and work.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Expansion work, or pressure-volume work, occurs when a system pushes back the surroundings, such as when a gas expands.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Bomb calorimetry is a method used to measure the energy content of foods by burning the food and measuring the energy it contains.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The Atwater system is a method used to estimate the energy content of foods based on the average caloric content of the different chemical constituents of food.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The combustion of glucose in a bomb calorimeter produces heat that is primarily absorbed by the water and the bomb, and the energy produced by the reaction can be calculated using the temperature change and the heat capacity of the calorimeter.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Nutritional labels on food packages show the caloric content of one serving of the food, as well as the breakdown into calories from each of the three macronutrients: proteins, carbohydrates, and fats.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Internal energy is an example of a state function, whereas heat and work are not state functions.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The value of a state function depends only on the state that a system is in, and not on how that state is reached.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Altitude or elevation on a mountain is a state function, as it does not depend on the path taken to reach the summit.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Enthalpy changes for chemical or physical processes can be determined, but enthalpy values for specific substances cannot be measured directly.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: To determine the amount of heat involved in a chemical or physical change, we need to consider the relationship between internal energy, heat, and work, represented by the equation ΔU = q + w.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The heat flow (qp) and enthalpy change (ΔH) for a process are equal when the process is carried out at constant pressure with the only work done caused by expansion or contraction.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The combustion of gasoline is a highly exothermic process, with the enthalpy of combustion of isooctane (a common component of gasoline) being -5450 kJ/mol.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Standard enthalpy of combustion is the enthalpy change when 1 mole of a substance burns (combines vigorously with oxygen) under standard state conditions.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The combustion of 1 mole of isooctane produces 5450 kJ of heat.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Algae can yield 60-90 gallons of biofuel per hectare.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The standard enthalpy of formation is an enthalpy change for a reaction in which exactly 1 mole of a pure substance is formed from free elements in their most stable states under standard state conditions.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The standard enthalpy of formation of O3(g) is 142.7 kJ/mol.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The standard enthalpy of formation of HCl(g) is -92.3 kJ/mol.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: To determine the enthalpy change of a reaction, use Hess's law: ΔH = ΣΔH of the steps.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The standard enthalpy of formation of an element in its most stable form is equal to zero.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The reaction of carbon with oxygen to form carbon dioxide can be thought of as occurring in two steps: C(s) + 1/2O2(g) → CO(g) and CO(g) + 1/2O2(g) → CO2(g).
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: According to Hess's law, the enthalpy change of a reaction is equal to the sum of the enthalpy changes of the steps.
