source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The reactivity of the alkali metals increases with increasing atomic number due to the ease of losing the lone valence electron.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The alkaline earth metals are less reactive than the alkali metals but still very reactive, and their reactivity increases with increasing size and decreasing ionization energy.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Zinc is an active metal that dissolves in hydrochloric acid, forming a solution of colorless Zn2+ ions, Cl- ions, and hydrogen gas.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Zinc has a lower reduction potential than hydrogen and will produce hydrogen gas when it reacts with acids.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Mercury is the only metal that is liquid at room temperature.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Most metals will form an amalgam with mercury, with the main exceptions being iron, platinum, tungsten, and tantalum.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Aluminum is amphoteric because it will react with both acids and bases.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The reaction of aluminum with a base depends upon the reaction conditions, with the following being one possibility: 2Al + 2NaOH + 6H2O → 2Na[Al(OH)4] + 3H2.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The group 13 elements have a valence shell electron configuration of ns2np1.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The inert pair effect refers to the formation of a stable ion with an oxidation state two lower than expected for the group.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Tin reacts readily with nonmetals and acids to form tin(II) compounds, indicating that it is more easily oxidized than hydrogen.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The principle tin ore is the oxide cassiterite, SnO2.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The preparation of sodium involves the electrolysis of molten sodium chloride using a Downs cell, with the reaction: 2NaCl → 2Na + Cl2.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The Hall–Héroult cell is an electrolysis cell for the production of aluminum.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Magnesium is isolated in large quantities by electrolysis.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The Pidgeon process involves the reaction of magnesium oxide with elemental silicon at high temperatures to form pure magnesium.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The metalloids are boron, silicon, germanium, arsenic, antimony, and tellurium.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The metalloids are semiconductors because their electrons are more tightly bound to their nuclei than are those of metallic conductors.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Silicon makes up nearly one-fourth of the mass of the earth's crust.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The crust is composed almost entirely of minerals in which the silicon atoms are at the center of the silicon-oxygen tetrahedron.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The purification of silicon begins with the conversion of impure silicon into silicon tetrahalides or silane, followed by decomposition at high temperatures.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Zone refining is used to purify silicon, where a rod of silicon is heated at one end by a heat source that produces a thin cross-section of molten silicon.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Pure silicon is necessary for the manufacture of semiconductor electronic devices.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Silicon reacts with halogens at high temperatures, forming volatile tetrahalides, such as SiF4.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Antimony reacts readily with stoichiometric amounts of fluorine, chlorine, bromine, or iodine, yielding trihalides or, with excess fluorine or chlorine, forming the pentahalides.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Boron and silicon halides can be prepared by the direct reaction of the elements.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Boron trihalides readily hydrolyze in water to form boric acid and the corresponding hydrohalic acid.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Silicon tetrachloride is a covalent tetrahedral molecule, which is a nonpolar, low-boiling, colorless liquid.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Silicon tetrafluoride can be prepared by the reaction of silicon dioxide with hydrofluoric acid.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Boron burns at 700°C in oxygen, forming boric oxide.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Silicon dioxide occurs in both crystalline and amorphous forms, with the usual crystalline form being quartz.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The contrast in structure and physical properties between silicon dioxide and carbon dioxide is due to the ability of group 4 elements to form strong π bonds.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Silicates are salts containing anions composed of silicon and oxygen, with sp3-hybridized silicon atoms occurring at the centers of tetrahedra with oxygen at the corners.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Nonmetals are elements located in the upper right portion of the periodic table, with properties and behavior quite different from those of metals on the left side.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Nonmetals do not form monatomic positive ions (cations) because their ionization energies are too high.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The first member of each nonmetal group exhibits different behaviors, in many respects, from the other group members, due to smaller size, greater ionization energy, and the fact that it has only four valence orbitals available for bonding.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Nonmetals form compounds with oxygen, yielding covalent oxides, and most of these oxides are acidic.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The acid strength of nonmetal hydrogen compounds increases from left to right across a period and down a group.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Graphite is a soft, slippery, grayish-black solid that conducts electricity due to its structure of layers of carbon atoms with each atom surrounded by three other carbon atoms in a trigonal planar arrangement.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Diamond is a very hard, colorless, and transparent crystalline material with a three-dimensional network of carbon atoms, each bonded to four other atoms at the corners of a tetrahedron.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Carbon nanotubes are carbon allotropes with a cylindrical structure, consisting of rings of sp2-hybridized carbon atoms, and they are extremely strong materials that are harder than diamond.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Phosphorus has multiple allotropic forms, including white phosphorus and red phosphorus, with different structures and properties, such as reactivity and toxicity.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Sulfur has a complex allotropy, with the most common and stable allotrope being yellow, rhombic sulfur, which consists of S8 molecules in a puckered ring structure.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Sulfur atoms bond to each of their two neighbors in a ring by covalent single bonds.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Nonmetals usually occur with eight electrons in their valence shells and form covalent bonds to supplement the electrons already present to possess an octet.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Hydrogen is the most abundant element in the universe.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Hydrogen is a component of more compounds than any other element.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Hydrogen is a colorless, odorless, tasteless, and nonpoisonous gas consisting of the diatomic molecule H2.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Hydrogen has three isotopes: protium, deuterium, and tritium, each with different names and chemical symbols.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The chemical properties of the different isotopes of hydrogen are very similar because they have identical electron structures.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: To prepare hydrogen, pass steam over coke (an impure form of elemental carbon) at 800°C to produce a mixture of carbon monoxide and hydrogen known as water gas.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: To produce additional hydrogen, mix the water gas with steam in the presence of a catalyst to convert the CO to CO2 and H2.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Hydrogen can be prepared by the electrolysis of water containing an electrolyte such as H2SO4.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The reaction of hydrogen with oxygen is a very exothermic reaction, releasing 572 kJ of energy per mole of water formed.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Hydrogen burns without explosion under controlled conditions.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The oxygen-hydrogen torch can achieve temperatures up to 4000°C due to the high heat of combustion of hydrogen.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Liquid hydrogen is an important rocket fuel, as seen in the space shuttle's main engines.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: An uncombined hydrogen atom consists of a nucleus and one valence electron in the 1s orbital.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Hydrogen can be considered a group 1 element because it can lose an electron to form the cation H+.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Hydrogen can also be considered a group 17 element because it needs only one electron to fill its valence orbital to form a hydride ion (H-).
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Hydrogen reacts with the metals of group 1 and with Ca, Sr, and Ba, the more active metals in group 2, to form crystalline, ionic hydrides that contain the hydride anion (H-).
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The reactions of hydrogen with nonmetals generally produce acidic hydrogen compounds with hydrogen in the +1 oxidation state.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Ammonia (NH3) forms naturally when any nitrogen-containing organic material decomposes in the absence of air.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The laboratory preparation of ammonia is by the reaction of an ammonium salt with a strong base such as sodium hydroxide.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The commercial production of ammonia is by the direct combination of the elements in the Haber process.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Ammonia acts as a Brønsted base.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The ammonium ion is similar in size to the potassium ion.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Ammonia can display acidic behavior, although it is a much weaker acid than water.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The nitrogen atom in ammonia has its lowest possible oxidation state.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Ammonia burns in air, giving nitrogen and water.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Chloramine (NH2Cl) results from the reaction of sodium hypochlorite (NaOCl) with ammonia in basic solution.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Hydrazine (N2H4) is a fuming, colorless liquid that has some physical properties similar to those of water.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Hydrazine is both a Brønsted base and a Lewis base, although it is weaker than ammonia.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Phosphine (PH3) is a gaseous analog of ammonia in terms of both formula and structure.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Phosphine is a colorless, very poisonous gas with an odor like that of decaying fish.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Hydrogen sulfide (H2S) is a colorless gas responsible for the offensive odor of rotten eggs and many hot springs.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Hydrogen sulfide is as toxic as hydrogen cyanide.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The reaction of a metal sulfide with a dilute acid produces hydrogen sulfide.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Hydrogen halides (HX) are binary compounds containing only hydrogen and a halogen.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The pure hydrogen halides are gases at room temperature.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The reaction of fluorine with hydrogen produces hydrogen fluoride (HF).
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Hydrogen fluoride is a weak acid, but it is highly corrosive and can etch glass.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Carbonates are moderately strong bases, and aqueous solutions are basic.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The reaction of carbon dioxide with the respective oxide or hydroxide produces carbonates of the metals of groups 1 and 2.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Nitrogen is a component of proteins and genetic material (DNA and RNA) of all plants and animals.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Nitrogen is very unreactive due to the strong triple bond between the nitrogen atoms.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Nitrogen-fixing bacteria can synthesize nitrogen compounds using atmospheric nitrogen gas as the source.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Nitrogen fixation is the process where organisms convert atmospheric nitrogen into biologically useful chemicals.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The only known kind of biological organisms capable of nitrogen fixation are microorganisms.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Microorganisms employ enzymes called nitrogenases, which contain iron and molybdenum, to fix atmospheric nitrogen.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Many microorganisms live in a symbiotic relationship with plants, with the best-known example being the presence of rhizobia in the root nodules of legumes.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Nitrous oxide is a colorless gas possessing a mild, pleasing odor and a sweet taste, and is used as an anesthetic for minor operations.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Nitric oxide is a colorless gas consisting of diatomic molecules and is one of the air pollutants generated by internal combustion engines.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Nitrogen dioxide is a deep brown gas that is responsible for the color and paramagnetism of the molecule, and is a strong oxidizing agent.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Dinitrogen trioxide is a blue liquid consisting of N2O3 molecules and exists only in the liquid and solid states.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Nitrogen pentaoxide is a white solid that is formed by the dehydration of nitric acid by phosphorus oxide, and is unstable above room temperature.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Phosphorus is an active nonmetal that usually occurs in oxidation states of +3, +5, and -3 in compounds.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Phosphorus forms two common oxides: phosphorus(III) oxide (P4O6) and phosphorus(V) oxide (P4O10).
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Phosphorus trichloride is a colorless liquid that is prepared by passing chlorine over molten phosphorus.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Phosphorus pentachloride is an off-white solid that is prepared by oxidizing the trichloride with excess chlorine.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Oxygen is the most abundant element on the earth’s crust.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: About 46% of the mass of the earth’s crust consists of oxygen, combined with other elements, principally silicon.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Oxygen occurs as O2 molecules and, to a limited extent, as O3 (ozone) molecules in air.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Oxygen is a colorless, odorless, and tasteless gas at ordinary temperatures.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Most of the oxygen isolated commercially comes from air and the remainder from the electrolysis of water.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The separation of oxygen from air begins with cooling and compressing the air until it liquefies.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Oxygen is essential in combustion processes such as the burning of fuels.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The administration of oxygen-enriched air is an important medical practice when a patient is receiving an inadequate supply of oxygen.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The chemical industry employs oxygen for oxidizing many substances.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: A significant amount of oxygen produced commercially is important in the removal of carbon from iron during steel production.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Liquid oxygen is important to the space industry as an oxidizing agent in rocket engines and as a source of gaseous oxygen for life support in space.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Oxygen is the final oxidizing agent in the reactions that provide energy for the maintenance of normal body functions in human beings and other organisms.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Green plants continually replenish the oxygen in the atmosphere by a process called photosynthesis.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The products of photosynthesis may vary, but in general, the process converts carbon dioxide and water into glucose and oxygen using the energy of light.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Ozone forms naturally in the upper atmosphere by the action of ultraviolet light from the sun on the oxygen there.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The presence of stratospheric ozone decreases the frequency of skin cancer and other damaging effects of ultraviolet radiation.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Chlorofluorocarbons (CFCs) caused depletion of ozone in the stratosphere.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The oxides of the representative metals can be categorized into three groups: oxides, peroxides, and superoxides.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The ease with which elemental oxygen picks up electrons is mirrored by the difficulty of removing electrons from oxygen in most oxides.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Aluminum oxide occurs in nature as the mineral corundum, a very hard substance used as an abrasive for grinding and polishing.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Zinc oxide is a useful white paint pigment, but pollutants tend to discolor the compound.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Lead dioxide is a constituent of charged lead storage batteries and is a powerful oxidizing agent.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: 2H2S + O2 → 2H2O + 2S
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: 2Na + O2 → Na2O2
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The stability of the peroxides and superoxides of the alkali metals increases as the size of the cation increases.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Hydroxides are compounds that contain the hydroxide ion.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Soluble metal hydroxides can be produced by the reaction of the metal or metal oxide with water.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Insoluble metal hydroxides form when a solution of a soluble salt of the metal combines with a solution containing hydroxide ions.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Most metal oxides are base anhydrides.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The chlor-alkali process is an electrochemical process that produces sodium hydroxide and chlorine.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Sodium hydroxide is an ionic compound that melts without decomposition and is very soluble in water.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Nonmetals react with oxygen to form nonmetal oxides.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Sulfur dioxide is a gas at room temperature and has a bent molecular structure.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Sulfur trioxide forms slowly when heating sulfur dioxide and oxygen together, and the reaction is exothermic.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Nonmetal oxides form acids when allowed to react with water, and the resulting oxyanions can form salts with various metal ions.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Nitric acid is a strong oxidizing agent and a strong acid, and it is stable in aqueous solution.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The Ostwald process is the commercial method for producing nitric acid, involving the oxidation of ammonia to nitric oxide, nitrogen dioxide, and finally nitric acid.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Nitrates are salts of nitric acid and form when metals, oxides, hydroxides, or carbonates react with nitric acid.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Sodium nitrite is an additive to meats such as hot dogs and cold cuts.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Nitrous acid reacts with certain organic compounds to form a class of compounds known as nitrosamines.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Nitrosamines produce cancer in laboratory animals.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Phosphoric acid is commercially available as a viscous solution known as syrupy phosphoric acid.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: One use of phosphoric acid is as an additive to many soft drinks.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Sulfuric acid is a colorless, oily liquid that freezes at 10.4°C.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Sulfurous acid is unstable and it is not possible to isolate anhydrous sulfurous acid.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The only known oxyacid of fluorine is the very unstable hypofluorous acid.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Perchloric acid is one of the strongest of all acids.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Sulfur exists in nature as elemental deposits as well as sulfides of iron, zinc, lead, and copper.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Sulfur exists in several allotropic forms.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The stable form of sulfur at room temperature contains eight-membered rings.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Sulfur exhibits a distinctly nonmetallic behavior.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The elements in group 17 are the halogens.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Halogens are too reactive to occur freely in nature, but their compounds are widely distributed.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The most common method for preparing fluorine is electrolytic oxidation.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Chlorine is also a product of the electrolytic production of metals such as sodium, calcium, and magnesium from their fused chlorides.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The commercial preparation of bromine involves the oxidation of bromide ion by chlorine.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Iodine is essential to the function of the thyroid gland.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Fluorine is the most powerful oxidizing agent of the known elements.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Fluorine reacts directly and forms binary fluorides with all of the elements except the lighter noble gases.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Chlorine is less active than fluorine, and oxidation reactions usually require higher temperatures.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Bromine is the weaker oxidizing agent and its reactivity is less than that of chlorine.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Iodine is the least reactive of the halogens and the weakest oxidizing agent.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The binary compounds of a metal with the halogens are the halides.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Most binary halides are ionic, but mercury, tin, and lead form covalent binary halides.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Interhalogens are compounds formed from two or more different halogens.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Fluorine is able to oxidize iodine to its maximum oxidation state.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The maximum number of smaller atoms possible in an interhalogen compound increases as the radius of the larger atom increases.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Examples of interhalogens include ClF, ClF3, ClF5, and IF7.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The noble gases are a group of elements in the periodic table that are unreactive and have filled valence shells.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The boiling points and melting points of the noble gases are extremely low relative to those of other substances of comparable atomic or molecular masses.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Helium is the only substance known that does not solidify on cooling at normal pressure.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Helium is used for filling balloons and lighter-than-air craft because it does not burn, making it safer to use than hydrogen.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Liquid helium is an important coolant to reach the low temperatures necessary for cryogenic research and to produce superconduction in traditional superconducting materials.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Nitrogen exhibits oxidation states ranging from -3 to +5.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Oxygen is one of the most reactive elements.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: All nonmetals except fluorine form multiple oxides.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The acidity of oxyacids requires that the hydrogen atoms bond to the oxygen atoms in the molecule rather than to the other nonmetal atom.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The strength of the oxyacid increases with the number of oxygen atoms bonded to the nonmetal atom and not to a hydrogen.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Sulfur reacts with almost all metals and readily forms the sulfide ion.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The halogens form halides with less electronegative elements.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The noble gases are inert and occur in low concentrations in the atmosphere.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The reaction of quicklime (CaO) with water produces slaked lime (Ca(OH)2).
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: To produce sodium metal, the electrolysis of sodium chloride (NaCl) is performed, and the resulting chlorine and sodium must be kept separate.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The metalloids have a maximum coordination number of four in their compounds.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Silicon has a diamond structure, but there is no allotrope of silicon with a graphite structure.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Nitrogen in the atmosphere exists as very stable diatomic molecules.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The reaction of calcium hydride (CaH2) with water can be characterized as a Lewis acid-base reaction.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Hydrogen forms only one bond in a covalent compound.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Carbon forms the carbonate ion, but silicon does not form an analogous ion.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Nitrogen does not form N4 molecules.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Ammonia can function both as a Bronsted base and as a Lewis base.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: To determine the oxidation state of nitrogen in a compound, review the chapter on chemical bonding for relevant examples.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The reaction of phosphorus with oxygen produces phosphorus oxide: P4 + 5O2 → P4O10
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Phosphorous acid forms only two series of salts, despite containing three hydrogen atoms.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: To determine the mass of phosphorus required to make a certain amount of phosphoric acid, use the equation: 2P + 5O2 → 2P2O5, then P2O5 + 3H2O → 2H3PO4
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Sulfuric acid is a stronger acid than sulfurous acid.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The reaction of sulfuric acid with sodium hydroxide produces sodium sulfate and water: H2SO4 + 2NaOH → Na2SO4 + 2H2O
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The halogens are strong oxidizing agents and tend to oxidize an element to its maximum oxidation state.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: To determine the molecular geometry of a noble gas compound, review the chapter on chemical bonding and molecular geometry.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Transition metals are defined as those elements that have or readily form partially filled d orbitals.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The d-block elements are divided into the first transition series (Sc through Zn), the second transition series (Y through Cd), and the third transition series (La and Hf through Au).
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The f-block elements are the elements Ce through Lu, which constitute the lanthanide series, and the elements Th through Lr, which constitute the actinide series.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The electron configuration of Ce³⁺ is [Xe] 4f¹, which belongs to the lanthanide series.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Lanthanides are fairly abundant in the earth’s crust, despite their historic characterization as rare earth elements.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The commercial applications of lanthanides are growing rapidly, with uses in flat screen displays, laptop hard drives, and alternative energy technologies.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Transition metals demonstrate a wide range of chemical behaviors, including the ability to form stable coordination compounds and react with nonmetals to form binary compounds.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The platinum metals (Ru, Os, Rh, Ir, Pd, and Pt) do not form stable oxyanions and have difficulty forming simple cations that are stable in water.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Transition metals can form compounds with a wide range of oxidation states.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The highest oxidation state of the elements scandium through manganese corresponds to the loss of all electrons in both the s and d orbitals of their valence shells.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The titanium(IV) ion is formed when the titanium atom loses its two 3d and two 4s electrons.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Iron is known to form oxidation states from +2 to +6, with iron(II) and iron(III) being the most common.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Most of the elements of the first transition series form ions with a charge of +2 or +3 that are stable in water.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The elements of the second and third transition series are generally more stable in higher oxidation states than those of the first series.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The atomic radius increases down a group, leading to the ions of the second and third series being larger than those in the first series.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Molybdenum and tungsten, members of group 6, are limited mostly to an oxidation state of +6 in aqueous solution.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: To determine the strongest oxidizing agent, look up the reduction half-reactions and compare the reduction potentials.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The permanganate ion, which contains manganese(VII), is the strongest oxidizer under acidic conditions.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The transition elements are extracted from minerals found in a variety of ores, with varying ease of recovery.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The isolation of iron involves three principal steps: preliminary treatment, smelting, and refining.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The preliminary treatment of iron ore involves crushing or grinding the ore, concentrating the metal-bearing components, and sometimes treating these substances chemically.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The smelting of iron ore involves the extraction of the metal in the molten state, including reduction of the metallic compound to the metal.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The refining of iron involves the removal of impurities, such as carbon, to produce steel.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The production of copper metal involves the roasting of copper sulfide ore, followed by smelting and refining.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The isolation of silver sometimes occurs through hydrometallurgy, which involves the separation of a metal from a mixture by first converting it into soluble ions and then extracting and reducing them to precipitate the pure metal.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The charges, as well as the atoms, must balance in reactions.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Oxygen is a good oxidizing agent for reactions because it can gain electrons to go from the -2 oxidation state to the 0 state.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Carbon is necessary to convert iron oxide into iron because it is converted into CO, which is the reducing agent that accepts electrons so that iron can be reduced.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The bonding in the simple compounds of the transition elements ranges from ionic to covalent.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Transition elements in their lower oxidation states form ionic compounds, while in their higher oxidation states, they form covalent compounds or polyatomic ions.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The variation in oxidation states exhibited by the transition elements gives these compounds a metal-based oxidation-reduction chemistry.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The stoichiometry of the metal halide that results from the reaction of the metal with a halogen is determined by the relative amounts of metal and halogen and by the strength of the halogen as an oxidizing agent.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Fluorine forms fluoride-containing metals in their highest oxidation states.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The polarity of bonds with transition metals varies based not only upon the electronegativities of the atoms involved but also upon the oxidation state of the transition metal.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Transition metal halides with low oxidation numbers form more ionic bonds.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The nature of bonding in oxides of the transition elements is determined by the oxidation state of the metal.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Oxides with low oxidation states tend to be more ionic, whereas those with higher oxidation states are more covalent.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The electronegativity of an element increases with increasing oxidation state.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Transition metals in low oxidation states have lower electronegativity values than oxygen, therefore these metal oxides are ionic.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The oxides of the first transition series can be prepared by heating the metals in air.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The oxides of the first transition series are not soluble in water, but they can react with acids and, in a few cases, with bases.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Basic metal oxides at a low oxidation state react with aqueous acids to form solutions of salts and water.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The oxides of metals with oxidation states of +3 are amphoteric, and most are not soluble in either acids or bases.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Vanadium(IV) oxide, chromium(III) oxide, and manganese(III) oxide are acidic and react with solutions of hydroxides to form salts of the oxyanions.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: A superconductor is a substance that conducts electricity with no resistance.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: High-temperature superconducting materials are typically oxides containing yttrium, or one of several rare earth elements, barium, and copper in a 1:2:3 ratio.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The new materials become superconducting at temperatures close to 93 K, temperatures that can be reached by cooling with liquid nitrogen.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: High-temperature superconductors can be used to generate magnetic fields for applications such as medical devices, magnetic levitation trains, and containment fields for nuclear fusion reactors.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Coordination number of the central metal ion or atom is the number of donor atoms bonded to it
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Transition metals do not normally bond in the same fashion as main group elements, instead they primarily form coordinate covalent bonds
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: A polydentate ligand is a ligand that bonds to a central metal ion by more than one donor atom
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The most common coordination numbers are two, four, and six, but examples of all coordination numbers from 1 to 9 are known
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: If a coordination compound is ionic, name the cation first and the anion second
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The complex [Pt(NH3)4Cl2] is named as tetraamminedichloroplatinum(II)
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The oxidation state of the metal is determined based on the charges of each ligand and the overall charge of the coordination compound
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: A chelate is a complex consisting of one or more polydentate ligands and a central metal
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The most common structures of the complexes in coordination compounds are octahedral, tetrahedral, and square planar
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Octahedral complexes have a coordination number of six, with the six donor atoms arranged at the corners of an octahedron around the central metal ion.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The nonbonding d electrons do not change the arrangement of the ligands in transition metal complexes.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Transition metals with a coordination number of four can adopt either a tetrahedral or square planar geometry.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The complex [Pt(NH3)2Cl2] has two geometric isomers: cis and trans.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Geometric isomers have different properties, such as color, dipole moment, solubility, and reactivity.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Optical isomers are nonsuperimposable mirror images of each other.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Optical isomers have identical properties, except for their ability to rotate plane-polarized light.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The complex [Co(en)3]3+ has optical isomers.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Linkage isomers occur when a ligand can bind to a metal center through two different atoms.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The ligand CN- can bind to a metal center through either the carbon or nitrogen atom, forming linkage isomers.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Ionization isomers occur when one anionic ligand in the inner coordination sphere is replaced with the counter ion from the outer coordination sphere.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The complexes [Co(NH3)4Cl2]+ and [Co(NH3)4Cl]Cl are ionization isomers.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Chlorophyll is a coordination complex that contains magnesium and is essential for photosynthesis.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Transition metals are used as catalysts in many industrial processes, including the production of sulfuric acid and plastics.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Coordination complexes are used as preservatives and in medical treatments for heavy metal poisoning.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The ligand EDTA coordinates to metal ions through six donor atoms and prevents the metals from reacting.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: British Anti-Lewisite is a drug developed to treat poisoning by heavy metals such as arsenic, mercury, thallium, and chromium.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Enterobactin is used to form complexes of iron and control the severe iron buildup found in patients suffering from blood diseases such as Cooley’s anemia.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Dimercaptosuccinic acid is used to treat heavy metal poisoning and has six possible donor atoms.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Cisplatin is a platinum complex used to treat certain forms of cancer.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Crystal field theory is a model that explains the behavior of transition metal complexes through electrostatic interactions between the electrons from the ligands and the electrons in the unhybridized d orbitals of the central metal atom.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The difference in energy between the eg and the t2g orbitals is called the crystal field splitting and is symbolized by Δoct.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The energy needed to pair up two electrons in a single orbital is called the pairing energy (P).
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Electrons will always singly occupy each orbital in a degenerate set before pairing.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The relative magnitudes of Δoct and P determine which orbitals will be occupied.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Complexes in which the electrons are paired because of the large crystal field splitting are called low-spin complexes.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The [Fe(CN)6]4- ion is a low-spin complex with only one unpaired electron.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: To predict the number of unpaired electrons, first determine the geometry of the complex and the number of d electrons.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The size of the magnetic moment of a system containing unpaired electrons is related directly to the number of such electrons.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The human eye perceives a mixture of all the colors in the proportions present in sunlight as white light.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: A coordination compound is a stable compound in which the central metal atom or ion acts as a Lewis acid and accepts one or more pairs of electrons.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The color of a coordination compound depends on the energy difference between the d orbitals of the central metal atom, which is influenced by the ligands attached to the metal.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Strong-field ligands cause a large split in the energies of the d orbitals of the central metal atom, resulting in a large crystal field splitting.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The iron(II) complex [Fe(H2O)6]^2+ appears blue-green because it absorbs photons in the red wavelengths.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The magnitude of the crystal field splitting (Δoct) depends on the nature of the ligands bonded to the metal.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The platinum metals are a group of six transition metals that tend to occur in the same minerals and demonstrate similar chemical properties.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The complex [Cu(NH3)4]^2+ is an example of a coordination compound with a tetrahedral geometry.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The lanthanide series consists of the elements with atomic numbers 58-71, which have valence electrons in f orbitals.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Organic compounds are defined as chemical compounds that contain carbon as the principal element, bonded to hydrogen and other carbon atoms.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The ability of carbon atoms to form up to four strong bonds to other carbon atoms results in a wide variety of organic compounds.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Hydrocarbons are the simplest organic compounds, containing only the elements carbon and hydrogen.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The number of potential organic compounds is estimated to be around 10^62, an astronomically high number.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Alkanes, or saturated hydrocarbons, contain only single covalent bonds between carbon atoms.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The bond angles in carbon chains are close to 109.5°, giving such chains in an alkane a zigzag shape.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Condensed structural formulas are a less detailed way to represent the structures of alkanes and other organic molecules.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: A skeletal structure, also called a line-angle structure, is a method used by organic chemists to simplify the drawings of larger molecules.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: To draw a skeletal structure, each carbon atom is converted into the end of a line or the place where lines intersect, and all hydrogen atoms attached to the carbon atoms are left out of the structure.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The chemical formula of a molecule represented by a skeletal structure can be determined by counting the number of carbon atoms and assuming each carbon atom has the number of hydrogen atoms required for four bonds.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: All alkanes are composed of carbon and hydrogen atoms, and have similar bonds, structures, and formulas.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Noncyclic alkanes all have a formula of CnH2n+2, where n is the number of carbon atoms present in the alkane.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Constitutional isomers are molecules with the same molecular formula but different spatial arrangements of the atoms in their molecules.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: To name an alkane, first identify the longest chain of carbon atoms in its structure, and then add prefixes to the name of the longest chain to indicate the positions and names of substituents.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: An alkyl group is obtained by dropping the suffix -ane of the alkane name and adding -yl
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Alkanes burn in the presence of oxygen, a highly exothermic oxidation-reduction reaction that produces carbon dioxide and water
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: A functional group is the part or moiety of a molecule that imparts a specific chemical reactivity
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Alkenes are unsaturated hydrocarbon molecules that contain one or more double bonds between carbon atoms
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Carbon atoms linked by a double bond are bound together by two bonds, one σ bond and one π bond
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Ethene (C2H4) is the simplest alkene, with each carbon atom having a trigonal planar structure
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Fractional distillation takes advantage of differences in the boiling points of the components of a mixture
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Unsaturated organic compounds contain one or more double or triple bonds between carbon atoms
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Polymers are large molecules made up of repeating units, referred to as monomers.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The variety of structures of polymers translates into a broad range of properties and uses that make them integral parts of our everyday lives.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Polyethylene is a commodity plastic used primarily for packaging, bags, and films.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Plastics are synthetic organic solids that can be molded, typically organic polymers with high molecular masses.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Most of the monomers that go into common plastics are derived from petrochemicals and are not very biodegradable.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The name of an alkene is derived from the name of the alkane with the same number of carbon atoms, with the suffix -ene indicating the presence of a double bond.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The reactant and product of the halogenation reaction of pentene can be named as cis-2-pentene and 2,3-dichloropentane, respectively.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Alkynes are hydrocarbon molecules with one or more triple bonds.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Alkynes are similar to alkenes, but typically react even more readily and react with twice as much reagent in addition reactions.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Aromatic hydrocarbons are a large family of hydrocarbons that contain ring structures and exhibit bonding that must be described using the resonance hybrid concept of valence bond theory or the delocalization concept of molecular orbital theory.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Benzene is the simplest member of the aromatic hydrocarbons, with a planar, hexagonal ring of sp2-hybridized carbon atoms.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Alcohols are derivatives of hydrocarbons in which an –OH group has replaced a hydrogen atom.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Alcohols do not behave like bases such as NaOH and KOH, despite having a hydroxyl group.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Ethanol is a particularly important alcohol for human use, found in wine, beer, and distilled drinks.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Large quantities of ethanol are synthesized from the addition reaction of water with ethylene using an acid as a catalyst.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Ethers are compounds that contain the functional group –O–.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: In the IUPAC system, the oxygen atom and the smaller carbon branch are named as an alkoxy substituent and the remainder of the molecule as the base chain.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Diethyl ether is a colorless, volatile liquid that is highly flammable and was first used as an anesthetic in 1842.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Carbohydrates are large biomolecules made up of carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen, with the general formula Cm(H2O)n.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Monosaccharides are sugars with one sugar unit, disaccharides are sugars with two sugar units, and polysaccharides are the polymeric version of sugars.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Fructose is a monosaccharide, while lactose is a disaccharide composed of two monosaccharides, glucose and galactose, connected together.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: An aldehyde is a compound that contains a carbonyl group bonded to at least one hydrogen atom.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: A ketone is a compound that contains a carbonyl group bonded to two carbon atoms.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The geometry around the carbon atom in the carbonyl group is trigonal planar.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The bond in a carbonyl group is polar due to the difference in electronegativity between carbon and oxygen.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: To prepare a carbonyl group, oxidize an alcohol by replacing a carbon-hydrogen bond with a carbon-oxygen bond.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Methane can be sequentially oxidized to form methanol, formaldehyde, formic acid, and carbon dioxide.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Formaldehyde is a colorless gas with a pungent and irritating odor, used for preserving tissue specimens and embalming bodies.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: A carboxylic acid is a compound that contains a carbonyl group with a second oxygen atom bonded to the carbon atom by a single bond, and the second oxygen atom also bonds to a hydrogen atom.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: An ester is a compound that contains a carbonyl group with a second oxygen atom bonded to the carbon atom by a single bond, and the second oxygen atom bonds to another carbon atom.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Carboxylic acids are weak acids, meaning they are not fully ionized in water.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Esters are produced by the reaction of acids with alcohols.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The ester ethyl acetate is formed when acetic acid reacts with ethanol.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The simplest carboxylic acid is formic acid.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Acetic acid constitutes 7-9% of vinegar.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Amines are molecules that contain carbon-nitrogen bonds.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Amines are weak bases due to the lone pair of electrons on their nitrogen atoms.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Amides are molecules that contain nitrogen atoms connected to the carbon atom of a carbonyl group.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The genetic material for all living things is a polymer of four different molecules, which are themselves a combination of three subunits.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: DNA (deoxyribonucleic acid) is an organic molecule and the genetic material for all living organisms.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: DNA is a double helix consisting of two single DNA strands hydrogen bonded together at each nitrogenous base.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Amines are organic molecules that contain an amine functional group, a carboxylic acid functional group, and a side chain specific to each individual amino acid.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The reaction between amines and carboxylic acids to form amides is biologically important, as it is through this reaction that amino acids link together in a polymer to form proteins.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: A peptide bond is a covalent bond formed between two amino acids when the carboxylic acid group of one amino acid reacts with the amine group of the other amino acid.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The formation of a peptide bond results in the production of a molecule of water, and reactions that result in the production of water when two other molecules combine are referred to as condensation reactions.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: A polypeptide is a chain of connected amino acids, and proteins contain at least one long polypeptide chain.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Enzymes function by lowering the activation energy of the reaction they are catalyzing, which can dramatically increase the rate of the reaction.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Kevlar is a synthetic polymer made from two monomers, paraphenylene diamine and terephthaloyl chloride.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Kevlar owes much of its strength to hydrogen bonds between polymer chains, which form between the carbonyl group oxygen atom and the partially positively charged hydrogen atom in the N-H bond of an adjacent monomer.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: An addition reaction is a reaction in which a double carbon-carbon bond forms a single carbon-carbon bond by the addition of a reactant.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: A carbonyl group is a carbon atom double bonded to an oxygen atom.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: To write the Lewis structure for a hydrocarbon, first draw the carbon skeleton, then add hydrogen atoms to satisfy the valence of each carbon atom.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The Lewis structure for ethane (C2H6) can be drawn by first drawing two carbon atoms connected by a single bond, then adding six hydrogen atoms to satisfy the valence of each carbon atom.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The hybridization of carbon atoms in saturated hydrocarbons is sp3, while in unsaturated hydrocarbons it is sp2 or sp.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: Nuclear chemistry is the study of reactions that involve changes in nuclear structure.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The nucleus of an atom is composed of protons and neutrons.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: A nuclide is a single type of nucleus, often referenced by the name of the element followed by a hyphen and the mass number.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The strong nuclear force is the force of attraction that holds the nucleus together.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The mass defect is the difference between the calculated and experimentally measured masses of an atom.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The nuclear binding energy is the energy produced when the atoms' nucleons are bound together.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The conversion between mass and energy is represented by the mass-energy equivalence equation, E = mc^2.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.

statement: The mass-energy equivalence equation is used to calculate the nuclear binding energy of a nucleus from its mass defect.
source_title: Chemistry 2e - P. Flowers, K. Theopold, R. Langley, W. R. Robinson, et al.
